text:
Title: Solid evolution of a classic shooter

After playing for more than 280 hours, I can safely say that Counter-Strike 2 is a worthy successor to CS:GO. The transition to the Source 2 engine has brought stunning visual upgrades, especially with the dynamic smoke physics and improved lighting that completely change the tactical landscape.

From a technical standpoint, the sub-tick system has become much more stable with recent updates, offering a smoother experience. While it requires a bit more from your hardware than the previous version, the optimization is constantly improving. Whether you are here for the competitive grind or just to "Rush B" with friends, the core gameplay remains as addictive as ever. Highly recommended for anyone who loves strategic, high-stakes shooters!

text:
Played Counter-Strike since 2014 when it was still CS. Watched it turn into CS2 in 2023, and somehow the game became worse in almost every possible way.

CS had cheaters too, but at least people still feared bans. In CS2, blatant hackers play like they own the servers because they already know nothing is going to happen to them anyway. Walls, aimlock, rage cheating — completely open, completely shameless, and completely normal now.

Every single session feels identical:
Queue.
Meet obvious cheaters.
Get frustrated.
Watch the system do absolutely nothing.
Repeat.

And somehow the people suffering the most are legitimate players instead of the actual hackers.

Trust Factor is probably one of the most broken and miserable systems ever forced into a competitive game. Nobody understands how it works, nobody knows how to properly fix it, and every answer sounds like random nonsense:
“Play more.”
“Keep grinding.”
“Queue with friends.”
“Don’t get reported.”

So after wasting dozens of hours playing miserable matches, losing game after game, and grinding nonstop trying to fix this invisible garbage system, it still randomly decides to throw you back into terrible lobbies anyway.

Recently played around 60 matches trying to improve the experience. Lost most of them. Finally saw yellow Trust Factor for a short time while queueing with a friend… and later the SAME DAY it went red again.

Absolute joke of a system.

At this point CS2 doesn’t even feel like a competitive game anymore. It feels like a matchmaking experiment where normal players are trapped suffering while cheaters run around freely ruining matches every single day.

VAC Live feels invisible.
Matchmaking feels terrible.
Trust Factor feels completely random.
And the overall experience feels more mentally exhausting than enjoyable.

This was my last game and I am deleting this game forever.

Thank you and ♥♥♥♥ you

text:
I really wanted to enjoy this game, but after spending a lot of time with it, I can honestly say it has been one of the most frustrating gaming experiences I have had. The game has potential, and that is probably the worst part, because you can clearly see that it could be something much better. Instead, it feels abandoned, unfinished, poorly managed, and focused more on taking money from players than actually improving the experience.

One of the biggest problems is the FPS and performance bugs. The game runs terribly far too often, even on systems that should be able to handle it. Random frame drops, stuttering, freezing, lag spikes, and unstable performance make the game feel broken. It is extremely annoying to lose fights or fail objectives not because of skill, but because the game suddenly decides to drop frames or freeze at the worst possible moment. Performance problems have existed for a long time, and yet they still feel like they have not been properly fixed.

Another major issue is that the developers do not seem to care enough about the actual problems players are reporting. Updates either take too long, ignore the biggest issues, or focus on things that do not really improve the core gameplay. Players have been asking for basic fixes for years, but many of the same problems are still there. It gives the impression that feedback is being ignored unless it is related to something that can make the game more money.

The lack of a proper anti-cheat system is also unacceptable. Cheaters ruin matches, destroy the competitive experience, and make honest players feel like they are wasting their time. A game like this needs strong anti-cheat protection, but instead, it feels like cheating is not taken seriously enough. When players constantly run into suspicious behavior and nothing meaningful happens, it makes the whole game feel unfair and pointless.

Player support is another huge disappointment. When something goes wrong, it feels like there is nobody there to actually help. Reports, complaints, and support requests often feel ignored or handled with generic responses. Good player support is important, especially in a game with so many bugs and issues, but this game makes players feel like they are on their own. If the developers want people to keep playing, they need to treat the community with more respect.

The maps are also a serious problem. Some maps still feel unfinished even after many years. There are areas that look incomplete, poorly designed, unbalanced, or simply neglected. After this much time, there is no excuse for maps to still feel like early-access content. Players should not have to wait year after year for basic improvements that should have been completed a long time ago.

Worst of all, the game gives the impression that it only cares about money. Instead of fixing FPS bugs, adding proper anti-cheat, improving support, finishing maps, and listening to players, the focus often seems to be on selling more paid content, cosmetics, passes, or other monetized features. It feels like the game is treated more like a cash machine than a project that the developers truly care about improving.

Overall, this game is disappointing because it could have been great, but it is held back by poor performance, lack of anti-cheat, weak player support, unfinished maps, and developers who seem more interested in money than fixing the actual game. I cannot recommend it in its current state. Until the serious problems are fixed and the developers start listening to the community, this game deserves criticism.

text:
CS2 has become one of the worst examples of corruption and neglect in competitive gaming. The game is completely infested with cheaters using hacks, wallhacks, aimbots, and every kind of blatant cheat imaginable, while VALVE and STEAM keep looking the other way.

It is impossible to believe that after so many years and millions of dollars earned, they are still unable to clean up the game. The reality seems very different: they do not want to do it because keeping those accounts active is profitable for them. As long as cheaters continue buying skins, trading items, and generating money through STEAM, nothing serious will ever be done against them.

Corruption has reached even the gaming industry. Legitimate players report cheaters for months or years, yet those same accounts remain active ruining matches every single day. VALVE and STEAM seem to side with hackers and cheaters instead of protecting honest players who only want a fair experience.

CS2 no longer rewards skill; it rewards whoever uses better hacks and is less afraid of getting banned. The anti-cheat system is a complete joke, and the competitive community has been destroyed.

After thousands of hours, I have decided to permanently quit this game. I will not continue supporting a platform that allows this level of garbage and negligence while they keep getting richer at the expense of normal players.

CS2 is dead for anyone who still believes in fair play.

I downloaded the game and purchased the “Premier” subscription.
I want to say that this game is full of cheaters and when I say full of cheaters, I mean more than 80% of the game’s community.
The developers and programmers are struggling to keep up with the growing number of hackers.
In fact, it almost feels like they benefit from this system, where cheaters pay for Premier, get banned, and then simply buy it again.
This clearly serves their financial interests, which is probably why they are not seriously dealing with the issue (this is only my assumption).

Honestly, I don’t think this game is worth playing despite its potential and the graphical improvements made over the years, because the overall experience is terrible.
It’s impossible to truly enjoy the game without encountering at least one or two matches per day filled with bots or hackers.

When playing Premier mode as a new player, you are likely to suffer through more than 10 matches with cheaters just to unlock the ability to see your Premier rating. Even then, hackers with ratings above 10,000 still exist in the game without getting banned.

To sum it up: the game can be fun, but I would not recommend paying anything for it because the overall experience is awful and unfair.

text:
CS2 is less of a competitive shooter and more of a social experiment to determine how much nonsense a human being can tolerate before uninstalling.

Every other match is a coin flip between obvious cheaters, people screaming slurs into their microphones, and teammates whose entire personality revolves around insulting women the second they hear a female voice. The community somehow manages to combine the maturity of a middle school Xbox lobby with the ego of aspiring esports pros.

You’ll also meet players who haven’t seen daylight in weeks, take every round like it’s the grand final of a major, and blame everyone except themselves after charging mid for the tenth time.

The gunplay is still great, which somehow makes all of this even more frustrating. Beneath the layers of toxicity, misogyny, and questionable anti-cheat effectiveness is a fantastic game desperately trying to survive its own player base.

10/10 gameplay.

2/10 community.

text:
After hours of mindlessly rotting my brain scrolling through YouTube shorts, I figured I’d dive into this game because I saw some German YouTuber losing his mind over opening cases and screaming "Gold gold gold!" Like that’s gonna be my reality. Spoiler alert: It isn’t! This game is like a brick wall for newbies. For your first few hours, you’re going to be queuing against 10-year CS2 veterans who’ve never seen daylight who hit nothing but headshots like they’re playing whack-a-mole.

And let’s not forget your teammates, who are equipped with all the awareness of a potato. Headphones? Nah, who needs those! You’ll find yourself screaming at them while they blissfully slide into death like it’s a slip ‘n slide. And before you even think about taking a bathroom break, think again! Step away for longer than 15 seconds and you'll be voted off the team faster than you can say “uninstall.” 

As if that wasn’t enough, the game is riddled with cheaters who can track you through walls. They act like I cant see them blatantly looking at other players through the walls. I spent over 100 hours on this delightful rollercoaster of despair before finally cracking and buying a case. After extensive lecturing, I was told, "not to open cases" because they "weren't worth it". But against all odds I decided to test my luck. Yeah, I'll never do that again, I got a well-worn skin worth less than the crumbs in my bean bag. Just kidding! I lied, I bought another case! Great! Now I have a gambling addiction...At least the game has a "great community," right? One last thing—the $10 prime subscription is a steal; you get free cases and skins every week that you can either sell or toss in the digital trash! 10/10 game!

text:
Counter-Strike is, without a doubt, the greatest game of all time for me. I've been playing Counter-Strike for over 20 years, and no other game has managed to keep me coming back for so long.

Over the years, I've met some amazing people through this game. Some of them became close friends, and a few I even met in real life. Very few games can create connections like that.

The gameplay is still fantastic. The movement feels responsive and satisfying, and the hit registration and feedback are usually instant and accurate. It's easy to get into, but incredibly difficult to master, which is one of the reasons why Counter-Strike remains relevant after all these years.

The maps are excellent, ranging from timeless classics to the occasional new and exciting community-made maps that keep things fresh. The graphics and the Source 2 engine are also a clear improvement and provide a modern look without sacrificing the core Counter-Strike experience.

Would I recommend Counter-Strike 2?

Honestly, I would — if it weren't for several major issues.

The cheating problem remains one of the biggest flaws of the game. Even worse, casual modes such as Deathmatch and Arms Race have become infested with bots to the point where they are practically unplayable at times. The overall selection of casual game modes is also surprisingly limited for a game of this size and age.

What makes this especially frustrating is the impression that Valve is either unwilling or unable to meaningfully address the cheating and bot problems. From a player's perspective, there seem to be very few effective measures being taken, while update after update focuses heavily on new skins and cosmetics.

What started as an incredible local multiplayer experience during my youth now feels like it's slowly crashing into the ground. Meanwhile, players are expected to get excited about the next case, sticker, or weapon finish.

Another thing that has changed for me is the whole case-opening experience. It simply doesn't feel rewarding anymore. What used to be an exciting little bonus has become something I barely care about nowadays. Opening cases no longer gives me the sense of anticipation or satisfaction it once did, making the constant focus on skins and cosmetics feel even more disconnected from the issues that actually affect the gameplay experience.

In the end, Counter-Strike 2 is still an amazing game at its core. The foundation is as strong as ever, and I will continue to play it. However, in its current state, I simply cannot recommend it to new players.

**Great game. Poorly managed problems.**

text:
Counter-Strike 2 has, despite the immense prestige inherited from its predecessor, become emblematic of the increasingly frustrating trajectory of contemporary competitive gaming, wherein aesthetic refinement is often prioritised over the preservation of mechanical consistency and player satisfaction. Although the title was ostensibly marketed as a revolutionary evolution of the franchise, many long-standing enthusiasts contend that it instead represents a perplexing regression in several critical aspects of gameplay. The subtick system, heralded as a technological innovation intended to enhance responsiveness, has frequently been criticised for introducing inconsistency into gunplay, thereby undermining the precise tactical integrity upon which the series originally built its formidable reputation. Furthermore, the game’s optimisation issues and irregular performance fluctuations have proven particularly aggravating for competitive players, many of whom rely upon unwavering frame stability and responsive input to perform effectively in high-pressure scenarios. Beyond the technical deficiencies, the transition from previous titles has also been accompanied by a pervasive sentiment that the game lacks the refined atmosphere and polished identity that once distinguished the franchise from its contemporaries. Maps that were once celebrated for their clarity and competitive balance have, in some cases, been visually overcomplicated, sacrificing readability for superficial graphical embellishment.
Moreover, the prevalence of cheating and inadequate anti-cheat measures has further eroded confidence within the community, fostering an environment in which legitimate players are frequently left disillusioned by encounters with blatant exploitation and suspicious behaviour. This frustration is exacerbated by the perception that communication between the developers and the player base remains sporadic and insufficiently transparent, creating the impression that community concerns are acknowledged only after prolonged public dissatisfaction. Economically, the game continues to place extraordinary emphasis upon cosmetic monetisation, with expensive weapon skins and cases often receiving more visible attention than substantive gameplay improvements, thereby reinforcing the cynical notion that profitability has eclipsed competitive integrity as the franchise’s foremost priority. Consequently, while Counter-Strike 2 undoubtedly retains moments of strategic brilliance and retains a dedicated global audience, it is increasingly viewed by critics and veteran players alike as a disappointing successor whose technical shortcomings, questionable design decisions, and persistent infrastructural issues prevent it from attaining the excellence once synonymous with the Counter-Strike name.

text:
Title: "What you see is what you get"... if what you want to see is absolute chaos.

Valve promised us the future of tactical shooters, but instead, they deleted CS:GO and handed us a broken tech demo.
​Let’s talk about the "revolutionary" sub-tick system. It’s amazing! I can now peek, run 3 meters behind a solid concrete wall, check my emails, and THEN die to a headshot from someone who didn't even see me on their screen. True magic.
​If you enjoy playing against spin-bots, flying accounts, and wizards who pre-fire you from across the map through three walls, Premier mode is for you. VAC doesn't stand for Valve Anti-Cheat anymore; it stands for Valve Allows Cheaters. The only thing getting banned in this game is my sanity.
​Optimization is a whole different joke. My PC used to run CS:GO at a smooth 300+ FPS. Now, every time a smoke grenade pops, my graphics card sounds like a SpaceX rocket taking off, and my FPS drops to PowerPoint presentation levels. The rubber-banding and random crashes aren't bugs; at this point, they are core gameplay features.
​But hey, at least the weapon finishes look shiny while the game is dying, right?
​Pros:
​Opened my eyes to how peaceful real life is when I uninstall it.
​Cons:
​Everything else.
​Rating: 0/10. Gabe, please sell the IP to a company that actually plays their own game.

text:
The current state of the anti-cheat system in Counter-Strike 2 is a critical failure that completely compromises the competitive ecosystem and completely negates all development efforts. The absolute incompetence and impotence of the protective algorithms have turned competitive matchmaking into a total farce. Instead of fair play, players face rampant third-party software usage that the VAC system is simply incapable of detecting.
Continuous failure to detect even the most obvious, blatant cheats demonstrates the fundamental structural inadequacy of the entire security architecture. The modified protection systems on Source 2, marketed as a cutting-edge technological barrier, have proven to be purely cosmetic and utterly useless. Infrequent and isolated ban waves do not solve the problem; they merely confirm negligence during network code design and the developers' helplessness. Valve's inaction destabilizes the platform and thoroughly destroys community trust. The current anti-cheat system completely strips CS2 of its status as a legitimate esport discipline, demanding an immediate, radical overhaul before the platform's degradation becomes irreversible.

continuous failure to detect even the most obvious blatant cheats demonstrates the fundamental structural inadequacy of the entire security architecture. the modified protection systems on source 2 marketed as a cuttingedge technological barrier have proven to be purely cosmetic and utterly useless. infrequent and isolated ban waves do not solve the problem they merely confirm negligence during network code design and the developers' helplessness. valve's inaction destabilizes the platform and thoroughly destroys community trust. the current anticheat system completely strips cs2 of its status as a legitimate esport discipline demanding an immediate radical overhaul before the platform's degradation becomes irreversible.

There is a secret that gives players no peace,
When your accurate shot goes into the void.

You aim for the head, the crosshair is like a magnet,
And you see how the enemy trembles from your bullet.
But here he stands, as if nothing happened,
And the blood on the screen is just an illusion.

"Register it, game!" you shout in your heart,
"My shot was accurate, I don't believe in words!"
But the server is silent, and the statistics lie,
And again resentment will grow in your soul.

You see how bullets fly through the enemy,
As if the walls are just your leg.
And it seems as if the world is a dream,
Where the rules of the game are just a strange law.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 has become a deeply alienating experience for the average solo player. The matchmaking system feels completely broken, frequently dropping English speakers into lobbies where communication is nonexistent and regional barriers are ignored. It creates a massive disadvantage in a game built entirely on coordination. Beyond the language gap, there is a toxic "gatekeeping" culture where pre-made groups treat solo players like uninvited guests, acting with an aggressive sense of ownership over every match.

The frustration is doubled by a total lack of accountability for trolls and smurfers. Because these players often queue in groups, the kick system is essentially locked, leaving you trapped in a ruined match with no way to fix the situation. At this point, the game has shifted from a tactical shooter to a weekly grind for drops. The only real incentive to keep clicking "Play" is the slim chance of landing an expensive case or skin to fund a different game on Steam. Valve has created a beautiful engine, but the actual social environment is a hostile landscape that only feels rewarding when the weekly loot finally hits your inventory.

text:
The reality is bitter but undeniable: the ban system in Counter-Strike 2 is completely broken. Cheaters are now running rampant with total impunity. To make matters worse, they have the nerve to play the victim and start crying the moment a legitimate player calls them out in-game.

The current state of the game can be summed up easily:

A toxic surge: The number of players victimized by blatant cheating grows every single day.

The mass exodus: Fewer and fewer real players are choosing to stay and endure this constant frustration.

A dark future: At this rate, the game will soon be entirely eaten alive by cheaters.

The ball is in Valve's court. Until they decide to take drastic, concrete action against this plague, the legitimate community will keep deserting. A monumental waste for a legendary FPS.

text:
Basically a full crap show

Today I downloaded few games that I lost and I downloaded the demos to see how people play on the winning side to learn something new. Well turns out that something new is long forgotten wall hack/aim bot and other stuff I don't how to call. Locking through walls, turning 180 degrees and preaiming before you can see the opposing player. Full tracking, dodging grenades without seeing them (matrix style) etc.
It's complete crap show from top to bottom. Deathmatch is even worse. In some lobbies 20 out of 26/28 people are bots. Some bots are with 360 fov headshot spam. So have fun. The market is also overrun by bot farmers to inflate prices. If you look at weapon count for most weapons the amount in existence is higher than active player count (which stands at 500K) by a factor of two at least. So how do those skins gain value? Bot farmers buy them all up, because you won't see most skins in the game any time. So where do they exist? In some bot farmers stashes.

And the worst part is that Valve encourages it actively. You can't hit financial targets by banning bots/cheaters. Can you? So this is how this goes. Bots farm and sell cases for gamblers. Then buy all play skins and pump stuff up in skin markets with the proceeds.  You can't see skins at 3000 elo, 5000 elo, 7000 elo, 10k elo, 13k elo, 15k elo. Sometimes you can meet a person with an inventory (not a well build inventory, but just an invetory), but they are far in between. So the A4M4 that should cost 2 USD, costs 30 USD and players end up paying bot farmers for their work. It's not wonder almost all manipulation comes from China, who runs bot farms.

I am not even going start about 128 tick vs sub tick garbage and all the problems that arise from that. Anyone who has worked with math and extrapolation knows, that extrapolation accuracy is non-existent, and never will be. If you don't believe me try investing on extrapolation. You will end up bankrupt ASAP. Shot bullets disappearing, not registering even in LAN. I saw ROPZ playing Lan and aiming straight in to the head of opposing player shooting and missing and that's lan... While completely missed shots land. While training with bots I encountered many times, where multiple bots run in a row, second and third bots die, but first one survives hit in the face from desert eagle. Worse is that I believe it's actually hardcoded into the game, the missing bullets I mean. If you shoot m4a4, m4a1, galil etc. you will encounter that shooting straight into the head, the second or first bullet disappears. Sometimes direct headshot from m4a4 the second bullet won't register headshot or disappear totally. So to score a headshot kill from m4a4 you need to shoot 3 bullets... There is a reason all Pros grab AK-47, the moment they can get their hands on it. If you shoot two bullets with AK47 into opponents head, one will register and it will be a kill. Not 3 bullets needed. How pathetic is that?

Verdict:
Don't recommend. Don't spend money on game until fixed.

text:
CS2 is the greatest piece of garbage ever created.

Every match is 45 minutes of questioning my life choices while being coached by a 14-year-old with a microphone that sounds like it's submerged in a bathtub.

My teammates have the tactical awareness of decorative house plants, yet somehow I continue to queue expecting different results.

I spend every game blaming Valve, my ping, subtick, my teammates, the economy, and occasionally myself if I'm feeling unusually accountable.

The enemy team's newest player has 12 hours on record and somehow flicks like he's being remotely controlled by NASA.

I have uninstalled this game multiple times.

I have written long paragraphs about how it's dying.

I have threatened to quit forever.

I played 8 hours yesterday.

CS2 is not a game.

It's a recurring psychological condition.

Hours played: 1,100+

Hours enjoyed: approximately 11

Would recommend: Unfortunately.

text:
They say "Leave the game to its demons" which perfectly encapsulates this one. Unless you want to drive your sanity and mental health to complete ruins, I wouldn't recommend even touching CS2.
Which leads me into further analysis of competitive free-to-play games. Take Valorant, Apex Legends, PUBG, Fortnite, Overwatch, CS2, ANY GAME as an example, and question yourself "Why do people keep playing them even though they don't get any satisfaction at the end of it?"
CS2 is a roller-coaster of emotions. One moment you indulge in complete euphoria from getting those juicy one-taps from the AK or DEagle. Then the next moment you're raging about not being able to hit a dude because he keeps on jittering around like a caffeine-drugged junkie.
The game constantly puts you up against stronger opponents whenever you win and against weaker whenever you lose, keeping you just at the edge of not quitting and not being bored. Thus, "Love-Hate relationship" begins to emerge between you and the game: you are left sucked up and beaten, promising yourself to stop playing this, but you're already way too deep into it; you're on a NEURAL level addicted to it - your brain already strung up a connection between getting a kill and releasing dopamine.
INSTANT GRATIFICATION of sorts. It's like jerking off or doomscrolling, i.e looking for a way to make yourself feel better, or chase after dopamine hit. In the end, you're left with nothing but a burned up image of yourself and a ♥♥♥♥ ton of wasted time because winning a game doesn't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ matter.
Ok I'm done rambling about ts.
This day marks the day that I leave the cuckchair.
♥♥♥♥ this ♥♥♥♥. I'm out.

text:
CS2 would be a good game if it weren't for the:
--hackers that seem to never get banned
--smurfs who only play in lower ranks
--ragers and griefers that never get banned
--edgelords who think yelling the N-word is cool
--a rating system that doesn't make sense and rises and falls based on your [i]team's[/i] performance rather than your own
--a report system that most certainly doesn't work
--movement that's clunky and unresponsive compared to 1.6
--shots that don't register for no particular reason
--footsteps that can only be heard when right next to you
--net jitter, whatever that means
--gambling game disguised as cosmetics crates, preying on young people
--general indifference to all of the above by the devs

If CS2 wasn't such a unique and popular tactical FPS with a strong esport following and amazing legacy from 1.6 and GO, it would be a fringe game not deserving of any attention.

text:
CS2 is truly an amazing game. I’ve never experienced so many emotions from a single game before. Thanks to CS2, I learned what anger, sadness, depression, and complete loss of faith in humanity really feel like. In just a few months, I developed high blood pressure, diabetes, lost my hair at 25, destroyed my sanity, and my family barely recognizes me anymore. My neighbors know every one of my rage moments, and even my monitor looks at me with disappointment.

I also lost half my friends, but at least now I know 17 different ways to swear in three languages. The community is incredible, the matchmaking is even better, and every game feels like a psychological experiment designed by demons.

But honestly… I’d still queue another match tomorrow.
10/10, highly recommended if you want to shorten your lifespan by at least 15 years.

text:
Valve's automated Griefing Ban system is a complete joke.

As a Level 10 FACEIT / 25k Premier player with nearly 3,000 hours in this game, I am extremely frustrated. I just got queued with a toxic 2-man premade stack who harassed me, body-blocked my crosshair, and dealt team damage the entire match. I didn't retaliate, I didn't type a single toxic word, and I instantly muted them to focus on trying to win.

My reward? A 24-Hour Competitive Cooldown because they stacked false reports against me.

Your system doesn't check demos, logs, or damage stats—it just blindly hands out bans based on report spam. Toxic stacks are literally using your own report button as a weapon to grief solo players. Fix this broken system, Valve. Stop punishing innocent players who actually love and grind your game.

text:
So yeah, after 230 hours in CS2, rocking a ★ Falchion Knife | Black Laminate (FT) and a bunch of other skins, I can say this: it’s not perfect, but it’s so much fun. 
The game feels tight and tactical—every round matters, and landing that clutch headshot never gets old.  Yeah, there are cheaters, especially in higher Premier ranks, but in the 230 hours I’ve played, I’ve mostly had solid matches.  The Source 2 engine makes smokes look insane, and the gunplay just feels better than CS:GO in a lot of ways—peeker’s advantage is real, but it balances out.
I play mostly Premier and Competitive, and honestly?  Casual mode is kind of a mess—it’s full of rushers and cheaters, but if you just want to mess around, it’s chill.  I’d love to see more fun modes like Arms Race or Danger Zone come back, but for now, the core 5v5 is still unmatched.
My Falchion Knife? Absolute banger. Looks slick, the inspect is fun, and it doesn’t cost a kidney like a Butterfly. Black Laminate hides the wear, and it just fits my style—low-key but deadly.

text:
Counter-Strike 2 (CS2) — это масштабное и визуально красивое обновление легендарного тактического шутера на движке Source 2, которое принесло как крутые инновации, так и новые проблемы.

Главным триумфом игры стал адаптивный дым, который теперь реалистично заполняет пространство, простреливается и разгоняется гранатами, полностью меняя тактику раундов. Графика, свет и читаемость моделей вышли на современный уровень, а матчи стали динамичнее благодаря переходу на укороченный формат до 13 раундов (MR12).

Однако переход на новый движок дался игре тяжело. Система sub-tick, призванная идеально регистрировать попадания, до сих пор вызывает споры в комьюнити из-за редких задержек, а сама игра стала гораздо требовательнее к железу, лишив комфортного FPS владельцев слабых ПК. Но самой большой проблемой остается слабый античит, из-за которого в соревновательных режимах все еще много читеров, что вынуждает хардкорных геймеров уходить на сторонние платформы вроде Faceit.

В итоге CS2 — это отличный, современный фундамент для будущего франшизы, который все еще находится в процессе полировки. Альтернатив такого уровня на рынке нет, но Valve предстоит проделать еще много работы над оптимизацией и безопасностью. Оценка: 7.5/10.

text:
[h1]I always used to say "I wish there was another game exactly like CS except it was actually fun"[/h1]

[spoiler]Years later I realized it's literally just going outside and living my life.[/spoiler]

[table][tr][th]I think Ive always circled back somehow, when living life becomes not so fun anymore, CS was always there to take care of this feeling I'd have about lacking in preparation or plans.

But somehow.. since I know nobody can ever be truly perfect at anything. I started sacrificing my body, organs, and mind, to the only thing in my life that was ever capable of letting me think I would eventually be "good enough"; but even then, I dont think it would have ever actually been me. 

My heart can't belong to something that doesn't care about how I feel.

So Counterstrike has always just been a distraction that made me think 
"maybe one day I'll deserve to feel like I'm enough"
rather than being something that was capable of ever making sure I knew that I always had been enough.

And I think that's why this game is so addicting. Because if I just escape everything one more time; maybe this time I will finally feel like I'm doing something right.

When Im playing a match, nothing about my life matters anymore except my ability to perform well.
There is literally not a single moment in a round that I can waste on thinking about anything other than what my next move is about to be.

So everything just disappears and something more rewarding takes its place; but only if I can execute it properly.

I think this can become really dangerous, because it effectively replaces every feeling required for emotional growth with something that makes you only feel positively about practical growth.

It is a drug. It's a drug that teaches you how to commit responsibily, how to work with others, how to see things other people might not, how to abstain from things you desire, how to find motivation in devastation...

But the only thing it will NEVER be able to teach you is how to implement how you truly feel about these things into who you are as a person.[/th][/tr][/table]

Maybe I just miss when it was my innocent escape from highschool rather than my devastating escape from how I feel about myself.

text:
CS2 has the foundation to be one of the best competitive shooters available, but Valve’s complete lack of urgency regarding cheating is destroying the experience for legitimate players. At this point, trying to take matchmaking seriously feels pointless. Reports rarely seem to matter, suspicious accounts continue playing without consequences, and far too many matches are decided by obvious cheaters rather than actual skill.

What makes it worse is that the core gameplay is still excellent, which only highlights how neglected the competitive environment has become. Instead of prioritizing anti-cheat improvements and protecting fair play, Valve appears far more focused on cosmetics and monetization while the integrity of ranked play continues to decline.

If you genuinely want a competitive experience where your time and effort are respected, you are probably better off playing something else until Valve starts treating the cheating problem seriously.

text:
200+ hours in and I can finally understand why Counter-Strike has remained popular for so many years.

CS2 is simple on the surface—plant the bomb, defuse the bomb—but the skill ceiling is incredibly high. Every match feels different, and there is always something to improve, whether it's aim, positioning, utility usage, or teamwork.

The graphics and lighting look great in Source 2, and getting that clean headshot never gets old. Premier can be frustrating at times, but winning a close match with friends makes it all worth it.

Still learning, still improving, and still queueing for "one more game."

200+ hours played and I'm nowhere near mastering it. Recommended.

text:
Counter-Strike 2:

Is a hard game for me to review, because my opinion has changed a lot over time.

When I was younger, Counter-Strike was easily an 8.5/10 experience for me. The combat, gunplay, teamwork, economy system, and round-based matches are still some of the best in any multiplayer FPS. It is simple to understand but hard to master, and that is what makes it so addictive. One good clutch, one clean headshot, or one close match can still remind you why this series became so popular.

CS2 also brings the game onto Source 2, with upgraded maps, better visuals, dynamic smoke grenades, redesigned audio/visual effects, and the move from CS:GO into the newer version. On paper, it is still one of the most important competitive shooters on PC.

The problem is that I do not enjoy it the same way anymore. The gameplay loop is still addictive, but it is also very repetitive, and the biggest issue for me is cheaters. In a game this competitive, cheaters can ruin the whole experience very quickly. When the match feels fair, CS2 can still be great. When it does not, it becomes frustrating fast.

The achievement side is almost not worth talking about anymore. Since CS:GO became CS2, there is basically just one achievement, so it is easy, but not really a proper achievement hunt.

I would recommend Counter-Strike 2 only to certain FPS players. If you enjoy competitive shooters and can handle the learning curve, repetition, and possible cheater problems, there is still a great game here. For me personally.

Overall: great combat and an iconic FPS, but hard to enjoy when cheaters ruin the match.

text:
This week I wanted to get a drop. I look at the missions, and there is a P90 mission in a community server, on a map called Warden. I had to go for 80 P90 kills. I go in, I dominate my opponents in 5 minutes. This guy votes me off, and now I cannot do my missions as I was voted off because I was hitting my shots better than him. Now, Valve does not allow me to continue doing my missions. After this I felt isolated and lonely, just because I played better did not mean I had to be verbally abused, kicked from a room. Simply from this experience I do not recommend this game, as the game does not protect the user from other malicious users.

Editing the Review

For the past two days now, I have encountered wall-hacking, aim-hacking, bunny-hopping players in Premiere matchmaking. I am now seriously considering uninstalling the game. I feel regretful to buy full price for this experience. I have reported the users multiple times in-game & on the steam profile reporting system. There was a cheater where they already had a VAC ban.

Considering the two instances above, I do not think I can recommend this game for a fresh user wanting to play multiplayer FPS.

text:
This game is a far cry from what CS:GO was in the past, but CS:GO wasn't perfect either. It had its own problems, like cheaters and bad voice chat moderation, but it also had a better engine (Source 1), faster movement, and better shot registration. If Valve focused on gameplay before skins and cosmetics, it is entirely possible for CS2 to be amazing with just a few adjustments.
It's frustrating to spray down a player, run behind a wall, and still die because of the 64-tick (subtick) system—it's bull**** and not fun. Visually, the game looks amazing, and I'm running 200+ FPS even with OBS and Firefox open, but they can still do so much better. In CS:GO, movement was indeed way faster, more skillful, and fun to use; here, it feels "noobified," slow, and built for newer players. Of course, new players are what keep a game alive, but this approach backstabs the older fanbase that has been here since 1.6.

The only thing I feel they did somewhat right was the empty magazine reload update, since it forces you to remember not to "Call of Duty reload." Other than that, Valve focuses too much on skins, and it feels like everything else is being thrown away or discarded. To be fair, I only started playing a year and a half before writing this review, so I only played a little bit of CS:GO. Still, GO felt much better—though not by a massive milestone like some people claim.
If they fix the anti-cheat, hit registration, and movement, I think more people will call this their "forever game." I hope Valve fixes these issues and rethinks the future of Counter-Strike altogether. We are not just numbers for your monthly income; we are people, and we should be heard and listened to more often. Without that connection, this game will become just like Overwatch 2: a game past its prime and heading downhill.

Want to take a step in any direction? Not only are you locked to a brisk jogging pace for the entire game, if not slower, but have fun shooting at the sun while youre aiming straight forward. How does such miniscule movement have such a ridiculous impact on the bloom in this game? Want to use an automatic weapon? Too bad you need to either tap fire or memorize recoil patterns in order to aim correctly. Three shots in and im shooting the f*cking moon. text:
Counter-Strike 2 Review: A Fresh Look at a Classic Formula
Overall Rating: 8/10

Counter-Strike 2 is a solid step forward for the legendary tactical shooter, though it definitely had some growing pains at launch. Moving from CS:GO to the Source 2 engine brought massive visual improvements, but the core gameplay we all love remains untouched.

The Good:

Beautiful Graphics: The maps look absolutely stunning now. The lighting is realistic, colors are vibrant, and everything feels much more modern.

Responsive Smokes: The volumetric smoke mechanics are a total game-changer. Being able to clear smoke for a second with a HE grenade or by shooting through it adds a whole new layer of strategy.

Sub-tick System: While it had a rocky start, the hit registration generally feels sharp and clean now.

The Bad:

Content Loss: It is still a shame that some casual modes and classic community maps from CS:GO didn't make the cut right away.

Optimization: The game is noticeably heavier on system specs than CS:GO. If you are playing on an older setup, you might experience some FPS drops.

Final Verdict:
CS2 is a beautiful and worthy successor. It is still the best game to grab some friends, hop on Discord, and play some chill casual matches or grind competitive. If you loved CS:GO, you will definitely enjoy this one once you get used to the new fee

text:
Executive Summary

Counter-Strike 2 (CS2) was marketed as a revolutionary leap forward for tactical shooters, built on the Source 2 engine. In reality, it has functioned as a forced, incomplete replacement for Counter-Strike: Global Offensive (CS:GO). By deleting a polished, decade-refined esport and replacing it with a fundamentally flawed product, Valve has downgraded the core gameplay experience, ruined competitive integrity, and alienated its most dedicated player base.
1. Technical Performance & The "Sub-Tick" Illusion

The foundation of any competitive tactical shooter is consistency and visual clarity. CS2 fails catastrophically here.

    The Sub-Tick Failure: Valve replaced traditional 128-tick servers with a proprietary "sub-tick" architecture. While advertised as calculating actions between ticks, the real-world result is severe desynchronization. Players routinely experience dying behind solid walls, delayed kill registration, and rubber-banding. What you see on your screen rarely aligns with what the server decides happened.

    Performance Degradation: The Source 2 engine introduced heavy particle effects (volumetric smoke, molotov fire) that cause massive frame-rate drops, even on high-end hardware. Micro-stutters and 1% low FPS drops make smooth tracking impossible, destroying the fluid movement that CS was famous for.

    The "Michael Jackson" and Animation Bugs: Hitboxes regularly desynchronize from character models, particularly during jumping, crouching, or rapid peeking, turning precise aiming into a lottery.

2. Total Collapse of Anti-Cheat and Competitive Integrity

Competitive matchmaking is entirely unplayable at high levels due to a total lack of effective oversight.

    The Non-Existent Anti-Cheat: Valve’s automated anti-cheat systems (VAC / VACnet) are a laughingstock compared to intrusive kernel-level competitors. Rage-hackers (spin-bots, wallhacks, instant headshots through doors) run rampant in the Premier matchmaking mode without consequence.

    The Third-Party Necessity: The fact that players are forced to download third-party software (like Faceit) just to experience a match free of blatant cheaters is a definitive admission that Valve’s core product is broken.

    Rank Inflation and Matchmaking Chaos: Premier rating points are deeply flawed, routinely matching solo-queue players against coordinated five-stack teams, resulting in stomps rather than competitive games.

3. Disastrous Casual & Community Support

Valve didn't just release a bad game; they actively stripped away content that players loved for years.

    Stolen Content: CS2 launched by deleting half of CS:GO's game modes. Missing maps, the removal of the iconic Arms Race (for months), Retakes, and Danger Zone stripped the game of its variety.

    Murdering the Community Server Browser: The community server browser—the literal lifeblood that birthed Counter-Strike through custom maps, surf, jailbreak, and zombie modes—is clunky, broken, and practically buried. By suffocating the community modding scene, Valve turned a diverse gaming ecosystem into a sterile storefront.

4. Prioritizing Gambling Content Over Gameplay

The development cycle of CS2 heavily implies where Valve’s actual priorities lie.

    Case Simulator with a Side of Shooter: Weapon finishes, knife skins, and cases function perfectly. While netcode, sub-tick fixes, and anti-cheat updates are delayed or ineffective, the steam market and case-opening mechanics remain flawless. The game operates more as a decentralized virtual casino than a competitive tactical esport.

Conclusion: The Final Verdict

Score: 3/10

Counter-Strike 2 is a massive step backward. Valve took a masterpiece of competitive balance (CS:GO), deleted it from existence so players couldn't go back, and forced an unpolished, cheater-infested, sub-tick experiment onto the community. It is a game that does not respect its players' time, competitive drive, or PC hardware.

Until Valve introduces a functional anti-cheat and fixes the desynced netcode, CS2 is an embarrassing regression that is not worth your time.

executive summary

counterstrike 2 cs2 was marketed as a revolutionary leap forward for tactical shooters built on the source 2 engine. in reality it has functioned as a forced incomplete replacement for counterstrike global offensive csgo. by deleting a polished decaderefined esport and replacing it with a fundamentally flawed product valve has downgraded the core gameplay experience ruined competitive integrity and alienated its most dedicated player base.
1. technical performance  the subtick illusion

the foundation of any competitive tactical shooter is consistency and visual clarity. cs2 fails catastrophically here.

    the subtick failure valve replaced traditional 128tick servers with a proprietary subtick architecture. while advertised as calculating actions between ticks the realworld result is severe desynchronization. players routinely experience dying behind solid walls delayed kill registration and rubberbanding. what you see on your screen rarely aligns with what the server decides happened.

    performance degradation the source 2 engine introduced heavy particle effects volumetric smoke molotov fire that cause massive framerate drops even on highend hardware. microstutters and 1 low fps drops make smooth tracking impossible destroying the fluid movement that cs was famous for.

    the michael jackson and animation bugs hitboxes regularly desynchronize from character models particularly during jumping crouching or rapid peeking turning precise aiming into a lottery.

2. total collapse of anticheat and competitive integrity

competitive matchmaking is entirely unplayable at high levels due to a total lack of effective oversight.

    the nonexistent anticheat valves automated anticheat systems vac  vacnet are a laughingstock compared to intrusive kernellevel competitors. ragehackers spinbots wallhacks instant headshots through doors run rampant in the premier matchmaking mode without consequence.

    the thirdparty necessity the fact that players are forced to download thirdparty software like faceit just to experience a match free of blatant cheaters is a definitive admission that valves core product is broken.

    rank inflation and matchmaking chaos premier rating points are deeply flawed routinely matching soloqueue players against coordinated fivestack teams resulting in stomps rather than competitive games.

3. disastrous casual  community support

valve didn't just release a bad game they actively stripped away content that players loved for years.

    stolen content cs2 launched by deleting half of csgo's game modes. missing maps the removal of the iconic arms race for months retakes and danger zone stripped the game of its variety.

    murdering the community server browser the community server browserthe literal lifeblood that birthed counterstrike through custom maps surf jailbreak and zombie modesis clunky broken and practically buried. by suffocating the community modding scene valve turned a diverse gaming ecosystem into a sterile storefront.

4. prioritizing gambling content over gameplay

the development cycle of cs2 heavily implies where valves actual priorities lie.

    case simulator with a side of shooter weapon finishes knife skins and cases function perfectly. while netcode subtick fixes and anticheat updates are delayed or ineffective the steam market and caseopening mechanics remain flawless. the game operates more as a decentralized virtual casino than a competitive tactical esport.

conclusion the final verdict

score 310

counterstrike 2 is a massive step backward. valve took a masterpiece of competitive balance csgo deleted it from existence so players couldn't go back and forced an unpolished cheaterinfested subtick experiment onto the community. it is a game that does not respect its players' time competitive drive or pc hardware.

until valve introduces a functional anticheat and fixes the desynced netcode cs2 is an embarrassing regression that is not worth your time.

text:
CS2. Обзор от того, кто уже смирился

Графон:
Всё красиво, ♥♥♥♥. Дымы теперь как настоящие, тени сочные, скины блестят. Только вот оптимизация — это отдельный вид пытки. На видосе у блогера 500 фпс, у меня на мощном пк — просадки до 30. Особенно на Инферно, карта красивая, но играется так, будто я на пекарне. Зато смотреть на обновлённые модели можно вечно... пока не начнется ♥♥♥♥♥♥.

Геймплей (он же "где мои попадания?"):
Стрельба изменилась, и не в лучшую сторону. Спрей из АК — это теперь лотерея. Вроде целишься в голову, а пули уходят в молоко. Сабтик обещали, что будет чисто, но на деле разница в пинге чувствуется так же, как и в GO. А ещё эти баги: пытаешься запрыгнуть на ящик, а моделька застревает. Или бросаешь дым, а он летит непонятно куда. И как в таком состоянии играть серьёзно?

Премьер-режим (ад для одиночек):
Отдельная песня. Рейтинг работает через жопу: за победу дают +100, за поражение с читерами снимают -400. А поиск игры... Ты сидишь, выбираешь карты, жмёшь старт, и ♥♥♥♥♥. Очередь зависает, потом ошибка перед баном карт, потом тебя выкидывает. А если найдет, то команда из пати против тебя одного и четырех раков, которые не знают, где кнопка "W".

Читеры (главная боль):
Их ♥♥♥♥♥. Серьёзно. В топе лидеров сидят аккаунты со 100% винрейтом. VAC Live, который должен был всех наказывать, работает как сито. Спинботтеры, валлхаки, аимботы — весь джентльменский набор на месте. Иногда кажется, что разрабы Valve сами продают читы, чтобы мы не расслаблялись.

Итог:
CS2 — это как старая любовь. Ты её ненавидишь за то, как с тобой обошлись, но каждую пятницу возвращаешься, потому что альтернативы нет. Вальва выпустили сырой продукт, и вместо того, чтобы допилить античит и нормальные серверы, они фиксят наклейки и ломают игру с каждым патчем всё больше. Оценка: 3/5. Играю, матерясь, и вам не советую. До следующего обновления.
Hard game at first, but is fun if you take time to learn it.

text:
Dear Gabe Newell, stop sending your undercover agents into my lobby. I am here to play a competitive tactical shooter, not to run an online orphanage for your mute, lagging, 4-digit-ping tourists who can’t get more than 8 kills in 30 rounds. If I wanted to carry dead weight on my back for 2 hours straight, I’d go work as a mover, not boot up CS2.

The worst matchmaking system in the history of competitive gaming. Valve has officially turned their competitive modes into a lazy casino and a hidden psychological experiment on solo players.
Your personal skill means absolutely nothing here. You can practice your aim, learn utility, clutch your brains out on overtimes, and drop 32 kills with a 1.45 KD — the algorithm doesn't care. It operates on a rotten scheme: if you play well, you get punished for your own skill. The game simply takes your high hidden MMR and uses it as a band-aid to fix lobby balancing for trash players who would otherwise uninstall the game.
You will be consistently, back-to-back, forced to carry dead weight — mute "tourists" with 4-digit ping and a pathetic 8/20 stat line who join Premiere with their e-partners just to flirt in a private Discord call. They will remain silent for 20 rounds, ruining your economy and positioning, mocking you in voice chat when you fail a 1v3 clutch, all while you are breaking your back carrying them. And to top it off, they will mass-report you for toxicity out of sheer helplessness and jealousy. Valve's system will blindly eat those reports, tanking your hidden trust factor and burying you even deeper into the swamp with the next batch of brain-dead bots.
To rank up here as a solo queue player, you have to grind through 100+ matches of pure burnout, because half of your games are pre-determined losses scripted by the system before you even accept the match. The developers actively choke skilled players to enforce an artificial 50% win rate, stretching your progress infinitely just to keep you hooked on the grid and buying their cases. Why would they fix the anti-cheat or build a fair system when digital pixels bring them billions every single night?
If you want competitive hype, transparent Elo, and actual discipline — run to Faceit. Premiere mode in CS2 is an absolute, unadulterated joke. 0/10 - Casino. 

Fix your ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ matchmaking or go fk yourself with your spineless cases, you greedy bastard.**

text:
In my opinion, Valve has lost interest in CS2.

While Deadlock receives major gameplay updates, reworks, new mechanics, and constant developer attention, CS2 feels abandoned.

The cheating problem continues to frustrate players, trust in VAC is at an all-time low, and many long-time fans feel ignored.

What makes it even worse is the growing feeling that Valve cares more about monetization than about the actual game. Some players believe that Valve would prefer more skin trading to happen directly through the Steam Market, where they collect fees, rather than through third-party marketplaces. Whether that's true or not, the lack of communication and focus on core issues has created a lot of distrust within the community.

Counter-Strike used to be Valve's flagship competitive title. Today it feels like the game exists to generate revenue while anti-cheat, content updates, and player concerns take a back seat.

Meanwhile, Deadlock gets the attention, innovation, and development effort that CS2 players have been asking for.

text:
Counter-Strike 2: A Disappointing Update to a Classic

Counter-Strike 2 was one of the most anticipated competitive shooters in recent years, promising improved graphics, better technology, and a fresh experience for fans of the franchise. Unfortunately, the game often feels more like an unfinished replacement for Counter-Strike: Global Offensive than a true step forward.

One of the biggest criticisms I have of Counter-Strike 2 is that it removed features and content that players had enjoyed for years in CS:GO. At launch, several maps and game modes were missing, making the game feel less complete than its predecessor. Many players expected a significant upgrade, but instead found themselves with fewer options.

The game's technical performance has also been a source of frustration. While the new graphics engine can make maps look more detailed, some players experienced lower frame rates and performance issues compared to CS:GO. In a competitive shooter where every millisecond matters, these problems can negatively affect gameplay.

Another major complaint is the matchmaking and anti-cheat system. Cheating has remained a concern within the Counter-Strike community, and many players feel that the game's anti-cheat measures have not improved enough. Facing suspected cheaters can make competitive matches feel unfair and discourage long-term play.

Counter-Strike 2's new "sub-tick" system was advertised as a revolutionary improvement for responsiveness, but many players reported inconsistent experiences. Instead of feeling like a clear advancement, the system became a topic of debate, with some arguing that gameplay felt less predictable than expected.

The game can also be criticized for relying heavily on the formula established by previous Counter-Strike titles. While maintaining a classic style is not inherently bad, Counter-Strike 2 introduces relatively few groundbreaking innovations. For some players, it feels more like a graphics update than a true sequel.

Conclusion

Counter-Strike 2 succeeds in modernizing the visual presentation of the series, but it falls short of the high expectations surrounding its release. Missing content, performance concerns, ongoing cheating complaints, and a lack of major innovation make the game feel underwhelming. While dedicated fans may still enjoy its competitive gameplay, critics could argue that Counter-Strike 2 is a frustrating example of a sequel that does not do enough to justify replacing the game that came before it.

text:
CS2 is just a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ circus on wheels. Valve released this piece of ♥♥♥♥, and playing competitive or Premier mode is pure self-torture. Every second match has some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ with a spinbot who doesn't even bother hiding it — he shoots you through the entire map while you're still in spawn. On your team, you either get silent vegetables or screaming ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ who yell into their mics and ♥♥♥♥ all over each other in voice chat instead of playing the game.

And don't get me started on the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ optimization – stutters, crashes, and tickrate that makes your bullets go absolutely nowhere near where you aimed. The only way to play without cheating scum is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Faceit. But that comes with its own pus-filled issues: their janky client, local 'pros' with egos the size of Everest, and you'll still get spam-reported just for landing a headshot through a smoke.

The most ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up part is the player base that crawls into this ♥♥♥♥. On one side — schoolkids with cheats who found some discount software for 3 bucks. They toggle spinbot right in the pistol round, but in voice chat they act all cocky: 'Git gud, old man.' On the other side — dudes in their 30s who should be paying mortgages and taking their kids to kindergarten, but instead they go buy private cheats with their ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ paycheck. Just to boost their Premier rank and feel important somewhere — like measuring ♥♥♥♥♥ with other pathetic old farts. Are you serious, man? You spent your own money on CS hacks instead of actually practicing or just enjoying the game?

And here's a special piece of ♥♥♥♥ — that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Prime upgrade for $15 (or whatever in rubles). I bought it, thinking there'd be clean matches, normal people. But what's the point? ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ NOTHING. The same exact cheaters play on Prime as on free accounts. This garbage gives ZERO advantage — absolutely nothing. You just flushed your money down the toilet. Thanks, Valve.

And on top of this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ circus, the skin market is crashing hard. Prices on knives and gloves are tanking because people are fleeing this dumpster fire. Your inventory that you've been building for years is losing value faster than Bitcoin at rock bottom.

But the worst part isn't even the money. I've spent fifteen hundred hours on this game. Fifteen hundred ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hours of my life. And now I clearly realize: that time was completely wasted. No fun, no growth — just constant frustration, cheaters, toxic shouting, and false hopes about the next patch that'll fix everything. It didn't fix a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ thing.

So in the end, we're witnessing a slow but steady decline. From the legendary CS 1.6, where skill and tactics were respected — to this beta version made by some incompetent devs who probably studied at a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ technical school in a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ neighborhood. A place where basic smokes disappear into the floor and the anticheat works on the principle of 'maybe it'll pass.' Valve, go ♥♥♥♥ yourselves with your 'successor.' This isn't CS. This is a piss-stained parody of itself. I regret every single hour and every single dollar. Don't fall for it like I did. This is a dumpster fire. Burn in hell.

text: 2k+ hours in to this game and 10k+ hours in to this franchise. This entire franchise is being subsidized by the in game gambling; i.e. loot crates & skins market. If the in game skins market wasn't worth millions of dollars, this game would have died 10-15 years ago. The actual gameplay is the same old and the competitive scene is more or less limited to pro leagues that take place on THIRD PARTY platforms like FaceIt. That's how bad the in game anti cheat is in this game, NOBODY that takes this game seriously plays competitively on it. Cheaters and hackers are so prevalent in game that third party platforms have their own market just based on superior anti cheat processes. This game is a sad, hollow shell of what it once was. And the developers know this; they just like making money because at the end of the day you have to buy this game (used to be $9.99) to even play on said third party platforms + millions in a market for in game skins. Very obviously, the developers spend more time and effort in creating new skins than working towards a fix for hackers in game or fixing the illogical matchmaking process. As a new player, you WILL go against more "smurfs" (experienced players that create new accounts or lose on purpose to derank to play in low level ranks to feel better about themselves) and hackers than actual other new players. This is because, as mentioned before, any experienced players that take this game seriously is not playing in game; they are playing on third party platforms like FaceIt. You will never achieve anything more than frustration trying to play this game standalone. Most players are usually in mod servers that aren't even related to the actual game, like zombie mods and zombie escape mods. This franchise used to be a leader in first person shooters, now its just a cash grab for developers trying to get as many people addicted to the in game gambling mechanics related to skins. Even as a free game, not worth your time, believe me. Unless you realllly think you wanna go "pro" for some odd reason or realllly like gambling.

text:
10/10 - Peak Gaming Experience, 0 Cheaters Whatsoever
Holy ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ♥♥♥♥, where do I even begin with this absolute masterpiece? Counter-Strike 2 is the pinnacle of competitive shooters. Every single match feels fair, balanced, and completely untouched by the greasy hands of spinbotting, wallhacking, triggerbotting Russian teenagers who clearly bought their accounts yesterday.
You know how they say "definitely no cheaters" in CS2? Well they're right. There are definitely no cheaters. The ones that are blatantly snapping to heads from across the map, pre-firing every angle like they have X-ray vision, and bunnyhopping through smoke while their crosshair is glued to your skull? Those are just "really good players," bro. Skill issue on your part. Git gud.
The Trust Factor system? Oh my god, it's not a complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ myth or anything. It's totally working. That's why I'm constantly getting matched with accounts that have 3 hours played, default knives, and a K/D ratio that would make Shroud quit in shame. Totally legitimate 12 year olds with 98% headshot accuracy who never miss a spray. Trust Factor is doing God's work, I can feel it in my soul.
I love dying around corners to people who shouldn't even know I exist. I love watching the killcam where the guy is literally 180 spinning while his viewmodel is doing the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Macarena. It's all part of the rich competitive tapestry Valve has woven for us.
Valve has single-handedly solved cheating by... well, they haven't. But they did add those pretty new smoke grenades and took away buy menu accessibility, so that's basically the same thing, right?
10/10 would get ♥♥♥♥ on by obvious cheaters again. Best $0 game I've ever rage-uninstalled and reinstalled the next day.
Valve, you beautiful bastards, never change.

text:
TLDR: CS2 is a no-no currently, lacks what CSGO had, community's infested with a major population of immature brats, problematics worming their way on both Casual and Comp modes, greed takes priority than the community, devs abandoned the game, and many other 99 problems we could list but am not going to tire myself with that.

Starting with the first and foremost cardinal advice, I recommend against playing Counter-Strike 2 in its current state.

It's the hollow promise that was never realized and also the reason that Counter-Strike Global Offensive deserved better than to be replaced by the game that's now left out to be severely rotten without any meaningful changes. The gameplay is as the same as you would've had it on Global Offensive, except this time it's practically turned into a barebones version that is not feeling the same. 2's Matchmaking modes are lacking where Global Offensive had much more on offer. You only have Casual, Deathmatch, Arms Race and Retakes. That, and the competitive modes such as Premier, Competitive and Wingman that I'm not ever keen on touching with a 10-foot pole because of the horrifyingly inconsistent anti-cheat that allows cheaters to illegitimately rank their way up through unprofessional means.

On Casual matchmaking modes, you'd be lucky to have some coherent individuals that know how to play the game, but chances are there are plenty of rotten apples who will go out of their way to completely sabotage your normal session and just attempt to votekick you out of the lobby because they suck and thrive off their bloated egos, or if you're playing way better than everyone on your team. Just because you happen to play better doesn't mean the immature runts have enough reason to do a votekick over it, yet they do.

Deathmatch? DON'T GET ME STARTED. Every map group has become infested with the botfarm problem that's been plaguing the mode for as long as it's still prevalent, yet nothing is done to outright use digital insecticide to brick the living daylights out of the lowly insect botfarm-operated accounts (with PRIME STATUS, mind you) that continue to worm their way into this mode for every weekly drop they can get.

Arms Race is just plainly stripped down two Gun-Game maps and two Shoots maps. Not worth the effort IMO, since it's just mindless gun-rolling and I don't care much for it.

Retakes is 4v3 straight-shot of the normal defusal mode but it's a genuinely 99% losing and 1% winning because you can't get teammates that constantly run into enemy players or sometimes get lucky if you have a team that aims to win. I even saw a duo of russian brats votekicking a singular teammate off the T side because they want to play duo and yet they lost because of it, that's how inept a harmfully immature majority of players are distinguished from the sane and mentally stable human players.

Like Global Offensive, you can have plenty of skins for your weapons, gloves, even agents to make you look distinguished from the default agents on every map because for some ungodly reason factions had to be removed and put back in as standalone agents that you have to buy. You can already guess that some high-tier skins and items even cost a lotta real world money and it's not feasible to save money on when you got cases that you have to buy untradable keys for.

The game may have stood solid in the beginning of its lifespan, but in the long-standing overtime it's worn out its welcome. No sane person should ever have to endure the majority of caustic dimwits within the playerbase who aim to sabotage your experience no matter the mode by being obnoxious and votekicking you for any reason to boost their bloated ego. And the fact that it's in a sordid state is inexcusable especially when greed takes priority within the high-ups and the devs have left the game and its players on life support.

text:
So I nearly played a thousand hours in this game and I'm ready to make my review, say my impressions and generally tell how i feel after playing it, hope it will help people who are reading this (There's a lot of things to say so prepare a drink or something in the meantime).

I never played CS:GO, source or 1.6 but after playing this game I kow the kind of enjoyment it procures for me, mecanically it's a great game, if you make a mistake it's on you, but learning how to counter strafe, peak, how to correctly place my cross-hair is something you want to strive for as you want to improve. Counterstrike is a mecanically difficult game but once you see how good or great players play you will understand step by step and how to generally understand what's happening in the many rounds you will have and how to make the best decisions for you and your teammates. The Pro scene is also incredible to watch and entertaining, to see how incredible players play and how they understand the game and apply their strategies, the decisions they make, some have a flashy style some have a more passive style but its fun and great to see and watch masters in their domain. I also like how simple yet difficult the game is, there is no drones, no special powers but you need good mecanical skills and coordination with your team (that many times fails) in order to improve and win rounds. I know that a lot of people will think wow this guys lives in a fantasy world outside the boundaries of reality dont worry we will get to the more darkest and worst parts of this game but since CS2 is my first person tactical shooter I still enjoyed the moments and strived to improve my skills, learn new utilities line ups in order to have more chances of victory ecc... 

As to why CS2 is for me a good yet controversial game is for 4 main reasons:

1- The game sometimes feels off and what I'm trying to say is that if you counterstrafe correctly with no issues or just shoot at the ennemy while your staying still placing your cross hair right into the head sometimes you will not get a confirmed kill, personally I don't have much of those issues but only sometimes, I'm sure its because I have a PC with good specs, but a lot of good players with good specs on their pc or not will tell the same thing about this. So I recommend to have good internet and a fairly good pc if you want to truly enjoy the game and not allways get frustrated. ( In this case it is not your fault if you did not won the duel against your opponent) I also personnally miss a lot of shots and throw a lot of opportunities.

2- The second issue is also about the game, sometimes the game will randomly lag and you'll experience a huge drop of FPS, sometimes the game wont start and you need to restart it or sometimes the game will slow you down with some bugs (cant charge 1v1 duels or join someone ecc.)

3- The third main reason is not about the game anymore but about the community that is also a bit controversial there is a part of the community that works a lot for the game to live on, with workshop maps where you can train yourself, warm up or even play a lot of different mods alone or with friends in special servers. But if you want to play premier (ranked) or competitive a lot of individuals are toxic or will just insult you just can't communicate with because they dont speak english. People who will lunch premier to only grief, team kill or hinder you. So I recommend that if you are the type of person to tilt or get angry easily then this game is not for you and its better to not play it for your mental health. Even though I met a lot of individuals like that I also met great people and laughed together and continued to play together so I recommend that if you are new to FPS play with an acquaintance who already plays this game it will help you a lot. 

4 - The fourth main reason is the anticheat that is clearly NOT WORKING, you can have in your games spinbots hackers, wallhackers who have every piece of info, aimbot hackers who will allways lock their target in your head no matter the situation, cheaters with bunnyhop scripts, cheaters with radar hack where they can know every move you do in the map and streamsnipers who also affects a lot the streamers community. This part of the game is really frustrating (but also brings happiness and joy when you beat them in many rounds a cheater or a team of cheaters because yes those who use cheats in CS2 are WASHED at the game) The game is basically infested with cheaters in any mode you want to play competitive, death match or premier. This problem is massive considering the importance of counterstrike and its playerbase and I know Valve has the funds and all the necessary things to make a good anticheat but they still decide to do nothing so I think its voluntary because there is no other way to answer that question as to why there is not a good anticheat. BTW you can check profiles of players to see if they are clean by typing "rip" or "tips" in the place of .com or "csteam" or "xsteam" at the begining of the link of the players account. This fourth reason is one of the biggest reasons why it leaves a bitter taste in my mouth and I'm starting to not enjoy as I enjoyed it in the beginniing so an advice from me is that the best thing to do is not take this game seriously even if you are playing ranked it is better to laugh it off instead of getting angry over people demeanor or cheaters. 

So dear readers I hope it will help you in your journey and made you think twice before learning russian and turkish insults (joke). Personally I still think it is a good game but has way too many issues to truly enjoy it and strive to grind the game, after discovering counterstrike and its history in the esport league I got compltely hooked and enjoyed watching majors and many differents pro leagues events and also I still enjoy the game a little bit. I do recommend that if you are the type of person who likes those aspects then welcome but to other people it is better for your bloodpressure and your sanity to not even start playing it. CounterStrike is like a beautiful goddess of insanity that whispers kind words into your ears trying to lure you in a sweet honey trap. The other controversial part is that in order to not play with or against cheaters you need to install another app called Face.it in order to play with no cheaters and have clean games. 

PS: I will not talk about skins and skins prices since it is a separate matter and of course careful guys while you are purchasing skins or cases, dont use money  you cannot afford to spend on gambling NEVER, also racism is not allowed or others different kinds of insults, be kind to other people even if they dont have a good behavior towards you or your team mates. More tan a tactical shooter it is a game of self control. 

Final result of this review do not recommend if you are a sane person.

text:
CS2 in 2026 is an absolute joke. It’s infuriating that we’re still dealing with the same fundamental issues that have plagued this game since launch.
  Let’s talk about the elephant in the room: the cheaters. It is frankly pathetic that a multi-billion dollar company like Valve can’t implement an anti-cheat that actually works. If FACEIT can maintain a system that catches 95% of these scumbags, there is zero excuse for VAC to be this useless. Every other lobby in Premier is a coin flip on whether you’re playing a game or watching a spinbotter destroy the lobby. It’s killing the competitive integrity of the game.
  Then there’s the "sub-tick" system. Valve keeps trying to sell this as "better than 128-tick," but it feels like hot garbage. Bullet registration is consistently inconsistent. You’ll have moments where you’re dead on target, spray control is perfect, and nothing hits. The desync is real, the movement feels heavy compared to CS:GO, and the overall "snappiness" that made CS a legend is just gone. 
  Valve is clearly more interested in pushing out skin updates and low-effort patches than actually addressing the core technical debt and the rampant cheating problem. They’ve turned a masterpiece into a chore. If you want to play a competitive FPS, go somewhere else until Valve decides they actually give a damn. Verdict: 2/10. Play it only if you hate yourself.
